title: Single-Candle Indecision: Doji and Spinning Tops
html:
The candles that say nothing — and why that matters

A doji is a candle whose open and close are essentially equal, so the body is a thin horizontal line. A spinning top is its softer cousin: a small body centered between two longer wicks. Both are indecision candles. They tell you that buyers and sellers fought for the whole session and ended in a draw. That is genuinely useful information — but only in context. By itself, a doji is one of the weakest standalone signals in all of technical analysis.

Let me be blunt about base rates up front, because most courses hide this. A doji appearing somewhere in the middle of a sideways chart predicts almost nothing; you would do no better than a coin flip trading it. The same doji at the exhaustion point of an extended trend, confirmed by the next candle, is a meaningfully different animal. Location is everything.

In the chart above, price has rallied hard and run well above its 20-day average. The doji near the top is not a sell signal on its own — it is a question. It asks: now that buyers have stopped making progress, will sellers show up? You do not answer that question. The next candle does.

Anatomy and the named variants

  • Standard doji — open ≈ close, modest wicks on both sides. Balanced indecision.
  • Long-legged doji — very long upper and lower wicks. Violent two-sided battle, maximum uncertainty.
  • Dragonfly doji — long lower wick, little or no upper wick, open/close near the high. Sellers pushed down, buyers reclaimed all of it. Mildly bullish at support.
  • Gravestone doji — long upper wick, open/close near the low. Buyers pushed up and were rejected. Mildly bearish at resistance.
  • Four-price doji — open, high, low, close nearly identical. Usually illiquidity, not a signal.
Definition.
A doji forms when |close − open| is a tiny fraction of the day's range, conventionally |close − open| ≤ 0.1 × (high − low). The smaller that ratio and the longer the wicks, the more genuine the indecision.

How to actually use them

Treat indecision candles as a trigger to pay attention, not a trade. The workflow is mechanical:

  1. Require location: the doji must sit at a meaningful level — a prior high/low, a moving average, a trendline, the edge of a range. A doji in no-man's-land is noise.
  2. Require a prior trend to reverse. Indecision is only informative when there was a decision in progress that is now stalling.
  3. Require confirmation: wait for the next candle to close in the reversal direction and ideally take out the doji's extreme.
  4. Place the stop beyond the far wick of the doji, where your thesis is wrong.
Reading a top doji — worked example
Setup: ACME rallies 80 → 132 over six weeks. Doji day: open 129.8 close 130.1 high 133.0 low 128.5 Body / range = |130.1 − 129.8| ÷ (133.0 − 128.5) = 0.3 ÷ 4.5 = 0.067 → true doji Confirmation candle closes below 128.5 (the doji low) → signal valid Entry short 128.3 Stop 133.2 (above doji high) Risk = 4.9 pts
Failure mode.
The single most common loss is trading the doji before confirmation. Indecision resolves in the direction of the trend more often than against it, so a naked doji-fade in a strong uptrend is a low-probability bet. Roughly speaking, an unconfirmed reversal doji works closer to one time in three; a confirmed one at a real level closer to one in two. Neither number justifies skipping the stop.
Insight.
A doji is a noun describing the market's state of mind, not a verb telling you to act. The verb is supplied by the candle that follows.
▸Try It

Build the discipline to ignore most dojis and respect the few that matter.

  1. Open any daily chart and mark every doji and spinning top you can find over the last year.
  2. Tag each one by location: in-trend, at a level (high/low/MA/range edge), or mid-range noise.
  3. For the at-a-level group, check whether the next candle confirmed a turn. Record the hit rate.
  4. Compare that hit rate to the mid-range group. Convince yourself with your own data that location and confirmation are doing all the work.

title: Hammer, Shooting Star and Reversal Candles
html:
One candle that rejects a price

Where the doji shows a stalemate, the hammer and shooting star show a rout — one side pushed price hard in one direction and got slammed back before the close. The long wick is a footprint of rejection. These are the most useful single-candle signals you will trade, but the same caveat from the doji lesson applies even harder: a hammer is only a hammer if it forms in the right place.

The shapes

  • Hammer — small body near the top of the range, long lower wick at least twice the body, little or no upper wick. Sellers drove price down; buyers reclaimed it all. Bullish after a decline, at support.
  • Inverted hammer — small body near the bottom, long upper wick. Buyers probed up and faded, but the bounce hints at demand. Bullish after a decline, weaker than the hammer, needs confirmation.
  • Shooting star — small body near the bottom of the range, long upper wick at least twice the body. Buyers pushed up and were rejected. Bearish after an advance, at resistance.
  • Hanging man — same shape as a hammer but appearing after an advance. The long lower wick warns that sellers are now testing. Bearish, needs confirmation.

Notice that shape and location together name the candle. A hammer shape after a rally is a hanging man; a shooting-star shape after a decline is an inverted hammer. The pixels are identical — the context flips the meaning.

Above, NOVA sells off into a support shelf near 88 and prints a hammer: the session traded well below 88 intraday, then buyers reclaimed the whole drop to close back at the top of the range. That is a textbook rejection of lower prices at a level that matters. The follow-through candle closing higher is what converts the footprint into a trade.

Formula.
Hammer test: lower wick ≥ 2 × |close − open| and upper wick ≤ 0.25 × (high − low) and body in the top third of the range. The mirror conditions define a shooting star.

Location and confirmation, made concrete

Compare two hammers of identical shape.

Same shape, opposite outcomes
Hammer A — at support after a clean downtrend range 86.0–91.2 body 90.6–91.0 lower wick = 90.6 − 86.0 = 4.6 body = 0.4 ratio 11.5× sits on prior support 88; next day closes 92.0 → confirmed long Entry 92.1 Stop 85.7 (below wick) first target prior swing 98 Hammer B — identical shape, mid-range, no level nearby no trend to reverse, no support under it; next day closes flat → no edge — skip it

Hammer A has a reason to work: price reached a level where buyers had defended before, rejected the breakdown, and confirmed. Hammer B is the same candle drawn in a place where rejection means nothing. Most losing hammer trades are Hammer B trades.

Failure mode.
The notorious failure is the hammer in a strong downtrend with no support beneath it — a falling knife. The long lower wick looks like a bottom, but the trend simply continues and your stop under the wick gets hit on the next leg down. Standalone hammers hit somewhere around 50–60% when confirmed at support, and far worse when faded into momentum. Always ask: is there a level here, and did the next candle agree?

Insight.
The wick is the story; the close is the punctuation. A hammer that closes at the very top of its range (a strong close) is worth more than one that closes mid-body, because the close shows who held the field at the end.
Do this.
Never enter on the hammer candle itself. Enter on a stop-order one tick above the hammer's high (for longs) so the market must prove follow-through before filling you. This single rule discards a large share of false hammers for free.
▸Try It

Separate location-backed reversals from cosmetic ones.

  1. Find ten hammers and ten shooting stars on daily charts.
  2. For each, mark whether a support/resistance level, moving average, or trendline coincides with the wick's extreme.
  3. Apply the confirmation rule: would a stop-entry beyond the candle have filled, and did it follow through five days later?
  4. Tally the with-level vs. no-level groups separately and see the reliability gap for yourself.

title: Engulfing Patterns and Morning/Evening Stars
html:
When two or three candles tell a cleaner story

Single candles are noisy. Multi-candle patterns are more reliable because they require a sequence of behavior — a shift in control that plays out over two or three sessions rather than one. The two workhorses are the engulfing pattern (two candles) and the morning/evening star (three candles). Both are reversal patterns, and both still want location and confirmation, but they start from a stronger base.

Engulfing patterns

A bullish engulfing is a down candle followed by an up candle whose body completely engulfs the prior body — it opens at or below the previous close and closes at or above the previous open. The market gapped against the bulls and they took the entire prior session back plus more. A bearish engulfing is the mirror: an up candle swallowed whole by a larger down candle.

In the chart, ACME retests its base and prints a wide up-candle that engulfs the prior down-candle near 91. The size matters: the bigger the engulfing body relative to recent candles, and the higher its volume, the more decisively control has changed hands. A bullish engulfing on heavy volume at support is one of the better two-candle reversal signals available.

Definition.
Bullish engulfing: candle₂ close > candle₁ open and candle₂ open < candle₁ close, with candle₁ red and candle₂ green. Compare bodies, not wicks. The cleaner the full-body engulf and the larger the size, the stronger the signal.

Morning and evening stars

The evening star tops a rally in three acts: (1) a strong up candle continuing the trend; (2) a small-bodied candle or doji that gaps up but stalls — the star of indecision; (3) a strong down candle that closes well into the body of candle one. The morning star is the bullish mirror at a bottom: big red, small star, big green.

The three-candle structure is informative precisely because it shows the hand-off: momentum, then hesitation, then reversal. That middle indecision candle is the tell. An evening star whose third candle closes below the midpoint of the first is more reliable than a shallow one.

Evening star — confirmation and risk
Candle 1 (up): 110.0 → 117.0 midpoint = 113.5 Candle 2 (star): small body around 118–119, gaps up, stalls Candle 3 (down): opens 118.0, closes 112.5 → below 113.5 midpoint valid star Penetration = (117.0 − 112.5) ÷ (117.0 − 110.0) = 4.5 ÷ 7.0 = 64% → deep, strong Entry short 112.3 Stop 119.2 (above star high) Risk 6.9 pts
Failure mode.
Engulfing patterns fail most often when they form mid-trend with no level or when the engulfing candle's strength is just a low-volume gap. Stars fail when the third candle barely penetrates candle one (shallow penetration = weak conviction). Honest base rates: a confirmed engulfing or star at a tested level reverses price meaningfully perhaps 55–65% of the time on the next swing; the same pattern in open space is close to random. Volume and penetration depth are your filters.
Insight.
Engulfing and star patterns already contain a degree of confirmation — the reversal candle has closed against the trend before you act. That is why they outperform lone hammers and dojis. You are not predicting the turn; you are reading one that has begun.
Do this.
Grade every engulfing/star on three axes before sizing: (1) is it at a level? (2) is the reversal body large and on rising volume? (3) for stars, did the third candle penetrate past the midpoint of the first? Three yeses gets full size; fewer gets a probe or a pass.
▸Try It

Calibrate your eye to penetration depth and engulf size.

  1. Collect five bullish engulfings and five evening/morning stars at swing highs or lows.
  2. For each engulfing, measure the engulfing body as a multiple of the prior body and note the volume.
  3. For each star, compute the third-candle penetration percentage into candle one.
  4. Rank all ten by your grades, then check which actually delivered the next swing. The strongest grades should cluster at the top.

title: Head and Shoulders Tops and Bottoms
html:
The most famous reversal — and one of the better ones

The head and shoulders is the chart pattern everyone has heard of, and unlike many famous patterns it actually earns its reputation when traded with discipline. It is a topping structure: three peaks, the middle one (the head) higher than the two flanking peaks (the shoulders), all sharing a common support line called the neckline. The inverse head and shoulders is the bottoming mirror — three troughs with a lower middle trough — and projects upward.

The structure and why it works

Read the story in the volume and the swings. Price makes a high (left shoulder) and pulls back. It rallies to a higher high (head) — but often on lower volume, a warning. It pulls back to the same support. It rallies again but only to a lower high (right shoulder), failing to confirm the uptrend's pattern of higher highs. The trend is now making a lower high; when price breaks the neckline that connected the two pullback lows, the uptrend is structurally broken.

Above, the neckline sits near 60. The pattern is not actionable while price floats above it — many would-be H&S tops never break and simply resume higher. The signal is the neckline break, ideally on expanding volume, optionally followed by a retest of the neckline from below that fails (the cleanest entry).

Formula.
Measured target = neckline − (head height) for a top, where head height = head price − neckline at the head. For an inverse pattern, target = neckline + head depth. Project the same vertical distance the head stood above the neckline, downward from the break.
Setting the neckline target
Neckline = 60 Head peak = 68 head height = 68 − 60 = 8 Target = 60 − 8 = 52 Entry on neckline break: short 59.5 Stop above right shoulder: 63.5 risk = 4.0 pts Reward to target = 59.5 − 52 = 7.5 pts R-multiple ≈ 1.9R Better entry: wait for neckline retest near 60 that fails → tighter stop, >2R

The example matches the skeleton: an 8-point head on a 60 neckline projects a 52 target. Note that the measured move is a typical objective, not a guarantee — many H&S patterns reach roughly the target, some overshoot, and a meaningful minority stall partway. Take partial profit into the target rather than expecting the full move every time.

Failure modes you must respect

Failure mode.
Three classic ways the pattern burns traders: (1) premature entry before the neckline breaks — the right shoulder fails to form and price runs to new highs; (2) the failed retest — price breaks down, you short the break, then price reclaims the neckline and squeezes you out (the retest that holds above the neckline invalidates the pattern); (3) no follow-through volume — a break on declining volume is suspect and frequently reverses. Studies and trader experience put a well-formed, volume-confirmed H&S in the rough vicinity of a 60–70% follow-through to a partial target, but unconfirmed breaks are far weaker.

The right-shoulder stop is non-negotiable. If price closes back above the right shoulder after you are short, the structure has failed and you exit — no averaging into a broken thesis.

Insight.
The neckline does not have to be horizontal. A gently up-sloping neckline in a top is common and still valid; a down-sloping neckline is weaker because the lows are already deteriorating. Draw the line through the two pullback extremes and break it on that line, wherever it points.
Do this.
Mark the right-shoulder high as your invalidation the moment you identify the pattern, and the neckline break as your trigger. Pre-commit to both prices in writing before the break so you are not negotiating with yourself in real time.
▸Try It

Practice mapping and measuring without anticipating.

  1. Find three head-and-shoulders tops and two inverse patterns on historical charts.
  2. Draw each neckline through the two pullback extremes; mark head height and project the measured target.
  3. Note whether the neckline break came on rising volume and whether a retest occurred.
  4. Check each outcome against the target, and log every case where the right-shoulder high was reclaimed — those are your invalidations.

title: Double and Triple Tops and Bottoms
html:
M-tops and W-bottoms: failure to make a new extreme

A double top is two peaks at roughly the same price with a valley between them — an M shape. It says the market tried to break a level twice and failed both times. A double bottom is the W-shaped mirror: two troughs at a similar low, signaling that sellers could not push lower. Triple tops and bottoms add a third test; more tests of a level make the eventual break more significant but also rarer.

The single most important rule with these patterns: they are not confirmed until the level between the two extremes breaks. Two equal highs are just resistance until price breaks the intervening valley low; two equal lows are just support until price breaks the intervening peak. Trading the second touch in anticipation is guessing.

In this double bottom, price bottoms near 40, rallies to a middle peak at 46, falls back to retest 40 (holding), then rallies again. Nothing is confirmed until price closes above 46, the middle peak. That break is the entry. Before it, you have only a pair of lows and a hope.

Formula.
Measured target = breakout level ± pattern height, where height = |middle peak/valley − the two extremes|. Double bottom: target = mid-peak + (mid-peak − bottom). Double top: target = mid-valley − (top − mid-valley).
Double bottom — confirm and measure
Two bottoms ≈ 40 middle peak = 46 height = 46 − 40 = 6 Confirmation: close above 46 Target = 46 + 6 = 52 Entry 46.2 on the breakout Stop 43.8 (below the retest low / mid-pattern) Risk = 2.4 pts Reward = 52 − 46.2 = 5.8 pts R ≈ 2.4R Optional: a pullback retest of 46 from above that holds = lower-risk add

This is the skeleton example exactly: a double bottom at 40 confirming on the break above the 46 middle peak, measured to a 52 target. The clean structure here gives you a defined risk (below 44) and a defined objective (52), which is the whole point of trading patterns — they convert a vague bullish feeling into specific prices.

What makes them more or less reliable

  • Separation in time. The two extremes should be days or weeks apart, not adjacent candles. A wide separation means two genuine, independent tests of the level.
  • Roughly equal extremes. The second top can be marginally higher or lower; a small overshoot that immediately reverses (a failed breakout) is actually a strong version of the pattern.
  • Volume. On a double bottom, volume ideally rises into the confirming breakout. A breakout on thin volume is the weak version.
Failure mode.
The dominant failure is anticipating the pattern — shorting the second top before the valley breaks, or buying the second bottom before the peak breaks. In a strong trend, the level often does not hold and the pattern never completes; you are then fighting the trend with no confirmation. Confirmed double tops/bottoms reach a partial target maybe 60–70% of the time; unconfirmed second-touch trades are far worse. A secondary failure is the breakout that fails to hold — the close back inside the pattern is your exit.
Insight.
Triple tops/bottoms are rarer and feel more reliable because the level held three times — but each additional test also chips away at the level. The third failure can precede a powerful break in either direction. Do not assume a triple top must reverse down; trade the confirmed break, whichever way it goes.
Do this.
Draw the confirmation line (middle peak or valley) the instant you suspect the pattern, and set a stop-entry order on it. Let the market trigger you. If it never breaks, you never traded a guess.
▸Try It

Drill confirmation discipline and measured targets.

  1. Find two double tops, two double bottoms, and one triple pattern.
  2. For each, mark the two/three extremes and the middle peak/valley that defines confirmation.
  3. Compute the measured target from the pattern height and note where you would place the stop.
  4. Verify whether the confirmation line actually broke and whether the move reached the target — and flag any that failed to hold the break.

title: Rounding Bottoms and the Cup and Handle
html:
Slow turns and the breakout that follows

Not all reversals are sharp V-bottoms or three-peak tops. Some are slow, patient curves where sentiment shifts gradually from selling to buying. The rounding bottom (a saucer) is the purest form, and the cup and handle is a refined, tradeable version that pairs a rounding base with a short consolidation before a breakout.

The rounding bottom

A rounding bottom forms when a downtrend decelerates, flattens out over weeks or months, and gradually accelerates back up — tracing a smooth U. Volume often mirrors the price: heavy as it falls in, light and dry at the bottom (capitulation and apathy), then expanding as it lifts out. There is no single trigger candle; the pattern is confirmed when price reclaims the left rim of the saucer, the high where the decline began.

Definition.
A rounding bottom is a gradual, roughly symmetric U-shaped base with no sharp swing lows, completing when price closes back above the left rim (the start-of-decline high). Target ≈ rim + depth of the cup.

The cup and handle

The cup and handle adds one crucial feature. After the cup's right side rallies back to near the old rim, price does not break out immediately — it drifts down in a small, tight pullback called the handle, shaking out the last weak holders. The breakout above the rim, out of the handle, on expanding volume, is the entry. The handle is what makes the pattern tradeable: it gives you a tight risk level and a clear trigger.

In the chart, ACME carves a deep, rounded cup over several months and forms a tight handle in the upper third of the cup before breaking out above the rim near 100. Quality cues matter: the cup should be rounded (a sharp V is lower quality), the handle should be shallow — drifting down no more than about a third of the cup's depth — and ideally form in the upper half of the cup. A deep, sloppy handle that gives back most of the cup is a warning, not a setup.

Cup and handle — entry, stop, target
Cup left rim = 100 cup low = 72 cup depth = 100 − 72 = 28 Handle range ≈ 100 → 93 (pullback 7 pts = 25% of depth) → shallow, good Breakout close above 100 → entry 100.5 Stop below handle low: 92.5 risk = 8.0 pts Conservative target = rim + depth = 100 + 28 = 128 Reward = 27.5 pts R ≈ 3.4R (depth targets are optimistic — scale out)

This matches the skeleton: a multi-month cup with a tight two-week handle breaking out on rising volume above the rim. The measured target adds the full cup depth to the breakout, but in practice the full depth is an optimistic objective — treat the first push as the high-probability portion and trail the rest.

Failure mode.
The cup and handle fails most often on (1) a breakout with no volume expansion — quiet breakouts above the rim frequently get sucked back in; (2) a too-deep handle that erases most of the right side, signaling the demand was not really there; (3) a V-shaped cup with no real base, which lacks the accumulation the pattern relies on. Even good cup-and-handles fail a meaningful share of the time, so the stop under the handle is essential. The pattern's edge is its favorable risk-reward, not a high hit rate.
Insight.
The handle's job is psychological: it removes impatient holders just before the move so there is less overhead supply to fight on the breakout. A handle that drifts gently down on declining volume is healthier than one that chops sideways on rising volume.
Do this.
Demand three things before buying a cup-and-handle breakout: a rounded (not spiked) cup, a handle in the upper half that retraces less than a third of the cup, and a breakout candle on volume clearly above its recent average. Missing any one downgrades the trade to a probe.
▸Try It

Learn to grade cup quality, not just spot the shape.

  1. Find three cup-and-handle candidates and one rounding bottom on weekly or daily charts.
  2. Measure cup depth and handle retracement; reject any handle deeper than a third of the cup.
  3. Mark the rim breakout level, the handle-low stop, and the depth-based target for each.
  4. Check whether the breakout came on rising volume and whether price reached even half the measured target — note how volume separated the winners from the failures.

title: Triangles: Ascending, Descending, Symmetrical
html:
Coiling ranges that resolve into trends

A triangle is a contracting range — price swings get smaller, the highs and lows converge, and volatility compresses until the pattern resolves with a breakout. Triangles are usually continuation patterns (the prior trend resumes) but can mark reversals, so the rule is the same as always: trade the break, not the build. There are three flavors, distinguished by which boundary is flat and which is sloping.

The three types

  • Ascending triangle — flat top (horizontal resistance) with rising lows. Buyers keep paying up while sellers defend one price. The pressure usually resolves upward. Bullish bias.
  • Descending triangle — flat bottom (horizontal support) with falling highs. Sellers keep pressing while buyers defend one price. Usually resolves downward. Bearish bias.
  • Symmetrical triangle — both boundaries sloping toward each other (lower highs and higher lows). Neutral; it resolves in the direction of the prior trend more often than not, but you wait for the break to know.

Above, NOVA presses repeatedly against a flat 30 ceiling while each pullback bottoms higher than the last. The rising lows show demand absorbing supply at progressively higher prices; eventually the 30 sellers are exhausted and price breaks out. This is the bullish bias in action — but the break above 30 on volume is the trigger, not the third touch of the trendline.

Formula.
Triangle measured move = breakout level + the triangle's height at its widest point (for an upside break). Ascending example: height = flat top − the first/lowest swing low; project that height up from the breakout.
Ascending triangle — measured target
Flat top (resistance) = 30 widest swing low = 26 height = 30 − 26 = 4 Breakout close above 30 → entry 30.2 Target = 30 + 4 = 34 Stop = below last higher low, ≈ 28.8 risk = 1.4 pts Reward = 34 − 30.2 = 3.8 pts R ≈ 2.7R

This is the skeleton example: an ascending triangle with a flat 30 top and rising lows breaking up to a 34 measured-move target. Note how tight the risk is — the last higher low sits just under the breakout, giving an excellent reward-to-risk that is a big part of why triangles are popular.

Reliability, timing, and the apex

Two timing rules sharpen the edge. First, the best breaks happen before the apex — roughly in the back half but not the final tip of the triangle. As price coils into the apex, the pattern loses energy and breaks become weak and choppy. Second, volume should dry up during the coil and expand on the break. A breakout on declining volume is the classic trap.

Failure mode.
Triangles are infamous for false breaks — price pokes through a boundary, traps breakout buyers/sellers, then reverses hard back through the triangle. This is so common that many traders wait for either a close beyond the boundary plus volume, or a successful retest of the broken boundary, before committing. Symmetrical triangles in particular break the "expected" way only modestly more than half the time. Even directional triangles (ascending/descending) follow their bias on the order of two-thirds of breaks at best. The narrow stop is what makes the math work despite the mediocre hit rate.
Insight.
An ascending triangle in a downtrend or a descending triangle in an uptrend is a counter-trend setup and less reliable than one aligned with the trend. The most trustworthy triangle is a continuation: an ascending triangle within an established uptrend breaking up.
Do this.
Draw both boundaries with at least two touches each, mark the apex, and only act on breaks that occur before the final quarter of the triangle and close beyond the line on above-average volume. Set the stop just inside the broken boundary so a false break ejects you cheaply.
▸Try It

Classify triangles and respect the apex-timing rule.

  1. Find one ascending, one descending, and one symmetrical triangle.
  2. Draw both boundaries, mark the apex, and note where in the triangle the eventual break occurred.
  3. Compute the measured target from the widest height for each.
  4. Check the break’s volume and whether it held or was a false break — and see how many resolved with the trend versus against it.

title: Flags, Pennants and Wedges
html:
Brief pauses inside a strong trend

After a sharp move, a trend often needs to catch its breath. Flags, pennants, and wedges are the short consolidations where it does so — small, brief patterns that interrupt a powerful move and then let it resume. They are the bread-and-butter continuation patterns of trend trading because they offer a clean entry to join a move already in progress with tightly defined risk.

Flags and pennants

Both begin with a pole — a steep, near-vertical price thrust. Then price consolidates:

  • Flag — a small rectangular channel that drifts against the trend (a bull flag drifts gently down, a bear flag drifts gently up). Two roughly parallel boundaries.
  • Pennant — a tiny symmetrical triangle, converging boundaries, also after a pole. Functionally a flag with a triangular shape.

The key feature: the consolidation is shallow, brief, and on declining volume. It should retrace only a modest part of the pole — not half of it. A pullback that retraces most of the pole is not a flag; it is a reversal in progress.

In the chart, ACME thrusts up (the pole), pauses in a tidy down-drifting flag on quieter volume, then breaks out the top of the flag to resume the move. The trade is the breakout from the flag in the direction of the pole, with the measured target adding the pole's height to the breakout point.

Formula.
Flag/pennant target = breakout price + pole height (bull) or breakout price − pole height (bear). Pole height = pole top − pole base. The flag "flies at half-mast" — it often forms near the midpoint of the entire move.
Bull flag — measured move
Pole base = 90 pole top = 106 pole height = 106 − 90 = 16 Flag drifts 106 → 102 (retrace 4 of 16 = 25%) → shallow, healthy Breakout above flag top 106 → entry 106.3 Target = 106.3 + 16 = 122.3 Stop below flag low: 101.5 risk = 4.8 pts reward = 16 pts R ≈ 3.3R

This is the skeleton example: a bull flag pausing after a sharp pole, breaking up to project the pole’s height added to the breakout. Flags resolve quickly — within roughly one to four weeks on a daily chart. A "flag" that drags on for months has lost its momentum and is really just a range.

Wedges — the trickier cousins

A wedge has both boundaries sloping the same direction (both up or both down) while converging. Wedges are counter-intuitive:

  • Rising wedge — both lines slope up but converge; momentum is fading even as price rises. Usually bearish — breaks down. As a continuation it appears in downtrends; as a reversal it tops uptrends.
  • Falling wedge — both lines slope down but converge; selling is decelerating. Usually bullish — breaks up.

The trap is intuition: a rising wedge looks bullish because price is rising, but the narrowing range and weakening thrusts betray exhaustion. Trade the break, which is typically against the wedge’s slope.

Failure mode.
Flags fail when (1) the "flag" is too deep — retracing more than half the pole means the trend may be over; (2) volume rises during the consolidation, signaling real distribution/accumulation against the trend rather than a pause; (3) the pattern drags on too long. Wedges fail when traders take the visually obvious direction instead of the break. Flags and pennants are among the higher-reliability continuation patterns when the pole is genuine and volume contracts in the flag — but a flat or trendless market produces fake flags constantly. No clean pole, no trade.
Insight.
The quality of the pole predicts the quality of the flag. A pole that is steep, on heavy volume, and breaks a prior level gives a flag worth trading. A weak, choppy run-up produces a flag that fails. Grade the pole first.
Do this.
Require declining volume through the flag and a volume pop on the breakout. Enter on a stop just beyond the flag boundary in the pole’s direction, stop on the far side of the flag, and target the pole height projected from the breakout — taking partial profit as price approaches it.
▸Try It

Tie continuation patterns to their poles and volume.

  1. Find two bull flags, one bear flag, and one wedge.
  2. For each flag, measure the pole height and the flag’s retracement percentage — reject any retracing more than half the pole.
  3. Project the measured target and mark the breakout entry and far-side stop.
  4. Confirm volume contracted in the flag and expanded on the break, and for the wedge note which way it actually resolved relative to its slope.

title: Gaps: Common, Breakaway, Runaway, Exhaustion
html:
Empty space on the chart, full of information

A gap is a void between one session’s range and the next — price opens beyond the prior bar with no trading in between, usually from overnight news, earnings, or a shift in sentiment. The gap itself is just a fact; the type of gap, defined by where it occurs in the price structure, tells you whether to fade it or ride it. The four classic types form a life-cycle of a move.

The four gap types

  • Common gap — occurs within a range or quiet drift, on no special news. It carries little meaning and tends to fill (price trades back over it) quickly. Fade-or-ignore.
  • Breakaway gap — occurs as price breaks out of a base or pattern on heavy volume, launching a new trend. These often do not fill and signal real commitment. Trade the continuation.
  • Runaway (continuation/measuring) gap — occurs mid-trend, as an already-moving market accelerates on fresh conviction. It often marks the rough midpoint of the move. Trade with the trend.
  • Exhaustion gap — occurs late in an extended move, a final lurch on climactic volume as the last buyers (or sellers) pile in. It tends to fill quickly and frequently precedes a reversal. Fade it — carefully, with confirmation.

Above, NOVA gaps up out of a long base on heavy volume and never looks back — the gap holds and a trend begins. That is the breakaway signature: gap plus base breakout plus volume, and crucially the gap is not filled. The unfilled gap becomes a support shelf; a later close back below it would be the warning sign.

Definition.
A gap fills when price subsequently trades back through the empty zone to the prior session’s close. Breakaway and runaway gaps that hold (stay unfilled) confirm the trend; gaps that fill quickly behave like common or exhaustion gaps. The volume on the gap day is the single best clue: heavy volume favors breakaway/runaway; climactic-then-fading volume favors exhaustion.
Breakaway hold vs. exhaustion fill
Breakaway: base top 92, gaps open 96, volume 3× average gap zone 92–96 stays unfilled → trend intact; trail stop under 92 entry on hold above gap 96.3 stop 91.5 (below base & gap) Exhaustion: after +40% run, gaps open 140, volume climactic price stalls, closes back below 140 open within 1–3 days → gap filling reversal signal once prior day’s low breaks; do not fade blindly into strength

This contrasts the skeleton example exactly: a breakaway gap out of a base that holds versus an exhaustion gap at the top that fills within days. The hard part in real time is that a runaway and an exhaustion gap can look identical on the day they happen. The distinction is only confirmed afterward — by whether the gap holds (runaway) or fills (exhaustion). Volume and how far into the trend you already are give you the probabilistic read.

How to trade gaps without guessing

  1. Locate the gap in the structure. Out of a base? Lean breakaway. Mid-trend? Lean runaway. After a long extended run on climactic volume? Lean exhaustion.
  2. Use the gap edge as your line. For continuation gaps, the gap should hold; a fill of the gap is the failure signal and your stop reference.
  3. Wait for the fade to confirm. To trade an exhaustion-gap reversal, do not short the gap itself — wait for price to fill back and break the prior day’s low.
Failure mode.
The two big mistakes: (1) fading every gap on the folk wisdom that "gaps always fill" — common gaps usually fill, but breakaway and runaway gaps often do not, and shorting a breakaway gap into a new trend is how accounts bleed; (2) buying an exhaustion gap, mistaking a climactic blow-off for a breakaway. The cure for both is volume and context: heavy volume on a fresh breakout is a friend; climactic volume after an extended run is a warning. There is no reliable base rate for "gaps" as a category — the type is everything.
Insight.
An unfilled gap acts as a magnet’s opposite — a support/resistance shelf the market remembers. Breakaway-gap supports often get retested and hold once before the trend continues, offering a lower-risk second entry for those who missed the gap.
Do this.
Tag every gap you see with a type the moment it forms, write your reasoning, then watch whether it holds or fills over the next three sessions. This single habit will teach you to read volume and location faster than any rule of thumb.
▸Try It

Classify gaps by structure and volume, then track their fate.

  1. Find five gaps across different charts: at least one out of a base, one mid-trend, and one after an extended run.
  2. Label each as common, breakaway, runaway, or exhaustion using location and gap-day volume.
  3. Mark the gap zone and note your continuation-or-fade decision with a stop at the gap edge.
  4. Track each over the next three to five sessions: did it hold or fill? Compare your labels to the outcomes and refine your read.
